questions.question: According to the cognitive model, what creates your emotional response to a situation?

options.text: The situation itself — external events directly cause emotions
Your interpretation of the situation — how you think about it
Your past experiences — emotions are conditioned responses
Your personality type — some people are naturally more emotional

questions.explanation: Correct. Your thought about the event creates your feeling. This is why CBT focuses on examining and testing your interpretations — changing the thought changes the emotion.

questions.question: Why does CBT focus on thoughts as the primary entry point for change?

options.text: Because feelings and behaviors don't matter as much
Because you can't change emotions or behaviors directly
Because thoughts are observable, testable, and changeable through practice
Because changing thoughts is easier than changing behaviors

questions.explanation: Exactly. Unlike emotions (which feel absolute and automatic), thoughts are mental habits you can catch, examine, and deliberately practice alternatives for. This makes them the most accessible leverage point for change.

questions.question: In the cognitive triangle, how do behaviors influence thoughts and feelings?

options.text: Behaviors don't influence thoughts — the arrow only goes one direction
Avoidance behaviors strengthen negative thoughts; engagement behaviors challenge them
Behaviors only matter if they're extreme — small actions don't influence thoughts
Behaviors change feelings but not thoughts

questions.explanation: Yes. When you avoid something (e.g., skip the party), your brain learns 'this is dangerous,' which reinforces anxiety. When you engage (go to the party), you collect evidence that challenges the fear. Behavior shapes belief.

questions.question: What is the difference between CBT-style thought change and 'positive thinking'?

options.text: There is no difference — both teach you to think optimistically
CBT focuses on realistic, evidence-based thinking; positive thinking forces optimism
Positive thinking is more effective than CBT for most people
CBT requires professional guidance; positive thinking can be self-taught

questions.explanation: Exactly. CBT teaches you to identify distortions (catastrophizing, mind-reading) and develop balanced thoughts that fit the evidence. If the situation is genuinely difficult, CBT helps you problem-solve — it doesn't tell you to 'just be positive.'

questions.question: Think of a situation this week where you experienced a strong emotion. Can you identify the thought that created that feeling? How might a different interpretation have changed how you felt and what you did?

title: The Cognitive Triangle: Thoughts, Feelings, and Behaviors - Quiz
